author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: the vortex is probably going to be made no matter what due to deformities in the manufacture of the pipe parts, so unless you get perfectly formed cylindrical shaped piping with concentric outside and inner diameters, as well as that elbow being perfect, the only way to eliminate the vortex, otherwise is to cut things into it to over power the direction of the gases caused by those deformities, (but seeing what direction it naturally goes might give a good idea on how to improve the exact vortex angle and portions it forms into so maybe you could use this to do some testing and analysis before say building a helion style fusion generator the  Trenta which If I recall you were talking something similar about extracting electrical energy directly from the fusion instead of using the heat produced to run a steam turbine right?

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: also if you can trust it to not break then obviously you can use that same stove for some light now, as well as heat and what ever implements you need to incorporate into it for say cooking or water heating and such. :) (be even cooler if you could get a clear pyrex glass container to boil that water in too HA HA.)

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: that is awesome, and the fact that you can see that motion of the flame to tell it is producing a vortex is exactly why I wanted to use all clear materials any time I can when building demonstration or testing and development kits for making generators and electric or magnetic motors. (that way people can see clearly what is going on and see there are no shenanigans in what ever it is.)

author: @dariusj33
text:
Nice one!!

Though I have a question. Why do you have to seal the glass pipe at the bottom junction? Wouldn't these gaps (if not sealed) act as an additional oxygen supply - like the breathing holes you made in the square stove? I'd probably make the chimney of a smaller Ø just to have some breathing gap at the bottom of it. Also, it would make it easier to flip the glass pipe after a burn and burn that soot off on the next burn → no need to shove your rag on a broomstick in and risk accidentally breaking the glass

Is my thinking wrong?

replies.author: @limmoblack
replies.text: A castellated gasket perhaps? Or leave gaps at intervals.

author: @cyrenecai
text: One nice practical effect of using a glass tube instead of steel is that you get all of the visible light output of the fire for illumination purposes, along with the heat... that just made me think of something.  I used to have a natural gas fueled street lamp in my front yard that used a mantle with thorium oxide that would glow really bright from the heat of the flame.  I wonder if a more robust version could be placed inside that tube above the flame to harvest more heat to specifically create light?

replies.author: @paulpvhl1930
replies.text: I've been wondering the same thing.

replies.author: @JB-pt6kd
replies.text: Limelight springs to mind. A lump of limestone becomes incandescent when a gas flame is played on it. Used in theatres and the occasional lighthouse in days gone by. Also an acetylene gas welding torch on a weak oxygen mix is a thing of wonder with a brilliant white light which motor vehicles used before electric bulbs were developed, they used calcium carbide reacting with water to generate acetylene gas. Strings of carbon drifting in the air were a sooty by-product.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
The Coriolis force works just as I knew it would, therefore, with a really smooth, straight, long tube, with very low pressure on the  trailing end, it fulfills all of the necessary conditions for producing a vortex.

This is some of the stuff I wrote about before:

It is mainly the two major known relevant aerodynamic factors that are producing the vortex: 

1, a drop in pressure, 

and 

2, a smooth surface without perturbances*. 

A third one, being a straight line of travel is not really all that important, but it helps some as well.

*[actually that sort of constitutes 2 separate rules, depending on which physicist you ask, but for this situation we shall leave it as only one rule (some people argue that the roundness is not equivocated with the smoothness, others will insist that they are one in the same thing for this application, I have no opinion either way, personally)]

author: @adamk2332
text: I've been subscribed and watching your channel for a long while now. I've seen seen SO many awesome things, including Luke's videos in my feed which, of course I watched.  In fact, I thought I was already subscribed to his channel. Imagine my horror, when I clicked on your link to his page, as an easy way of seeing what he had new... And I saw the subscribe button present, with no "d" at the end of the word. Lol. I immediately remedied the situation and am happily following a great uncle-nephew duo. Thank you both for the entertainment through learning, not only from, but sometimes in a sense, right along with you. You both, genuinely, have a way of, instead of trying to invade our screens and homes, making  it seem like  you are here with us, you make us feel like we're there with you. Part of the "experiment, build, test, smile, chuckle, repeat" process that science needs, is exactly what you deliver. I, thank you gentlemen from the bottom of my heart, and from every fold of my brain, which you have, most assuredly, enhanced.

author: @jamest.5001
text: That would be awesome if it was about 10" diameter, glass tube, and use a steel screen wire  tube inside to get the swirl,  keeping the flame from the glass, keeping the carbon off the glass, just cut a steel plate with a hole to fit the stove in the middle. About 1" larger than the glass tube, 11" then roll a piece of 2 " flat bar, to fit the od of the glass tube, weld it to the plate, make another with about 4" to 6" legs on it, to space it up higher to brace the tube, maybe drill 1/2" holes in groups of lines to let air in, and possibly add to the swirl,  the stainless screen will keep the fire away from glass, maybe it could be done with the glass you have, just use a smaller screen, about 1" smaller than the glass, it may prevent hot spots also, that can break the tube, maybe put a screen around the outside also, Incase it does break, then also it may block some cold air from causing the glass to break,  by blocking, and  by heating the air that blows through it possibly enough to preventing a thermal shock! ✌️🕊️

author: @handyhippie6548
text: if that glass tube were surrounded by a heavy wire mesh to contain the fragments should it break, that would be an awesome device. the red colour of the glass at the base was a tad troubling. i have tried my hand at borosilicate glass blowing, and that red is an indication that the tube is nearing the temp where glass begins to become more plastic, and it may buckle and/or collapse due to the weight of the tube above, as the trade center towers in new york did when they were hit by the hijacked planes. it wasn't the plane crashes that brought them down, but was due to the steel beams being unable to support the upper structure after being subjected to the intense heat of the burning jet fuel. semi-molten glass fragments spewing across the immediate area is the very definition of a bad day, and i have a scar or two to prove it.

author: @patrick1502

author: @meredithromo6353
text: Hi Robert,  Rocket stove + sand battery.  In terms of home heating, it would be nice to find out how much heat could be transferred from a rocket stove this size run for an hour piped through a 55 gallon drum of sand.  Efficient or a waste of time?  I've seen a lot of rocket stove/cob heaters, but I know the cob breaks down in short order and clean out under a cob bench isn't easy either.  Love your videos.  Keep em' comin'.  Thanks for all you do.

replies.author: @johndough8115
replies.text:
You are talking two different things.  Rocket stove vs Rocket mass heater.  This is basically just a stove, and so virtually all of the heat goes Upwards.  Even if you surrounded the vertical pipe with sand... its not going to capture that much of the heat.    Where as a rocket mass heater, exchanges the bulk of the heat at top of a barrel... and the rest is captured and released slower.. through mass, snaked through a long horizonal tubing section.

 The longer you can keep the heat horizontal through the exhaust pipe... the more likely it will be able to capture and transfer all of that heat, to the  sand/mass.
But again... if you are just using a vertical only exhaust... you are going to lose the majority of the heat,  into  "Instant" upward heat,  rather than slow-release mass based heat.

 Now... if you mean, to put a 55 gallon drum of sand, directly AOVE this stove... then thats different.  In that case, you would be collecting the majority of the fast upwards rising heat.  A lot will still spill out the sides, around the barrel..  but at least the majority will go right into the center of the barrel (and any hot air that goes around the sides, will also help keep the barrels exterior heated).   The biggest issue, besides total height and weight (that much sand would be VERY heavy!)... would be the sustained heat levels on the barrels steel bottom.   The rocket stove might burn a hole in the barrel, in a months time.   In fact it would probably be best to use a quarter inch thick steel plate for the contact point of the flame (which still might have to be replaced every year or two.. depending on how how the thing gets).

replies.author: @meredithromo6353
replies.text:
@johndough8115 Thanks for the detailed explanation.  The only 'approved' rocket stove for home heating I've seen is the 'Liberator' Robert reviewed a week or two ago, which isn't a real mass heater.  Rocket mass heaters don't meet code requirements and will void home insurance policies.  What I pictured was a rocket stove feeding into a couple horizontally placed insulated 55 gallon drums and then exhausted up…but everything outside the home.  The stored heat could then be vented into the house.

You're absolutely right about the heat damage rocket stoves can do to metal.  I noticed the 'Liberator' is now lining their exhaust with ceramic or fire brick.  I believe Robert misread this as for a better burn, but I think it is more to protect the metal.  I know Robert only scans his hundreds of replies so I didn't expect any response.  Just hoping to trigger another video.

FYI  If thinking of doing a RMH, one thing seldom mentioned is that due to the long exhaust, sometimes you have to preheat the end of the chimney or it will not draw properly.  Then you have a house full of smoke.  

Probably the most successful guy I've seen with rocket stoves is on Youtube.  Do rocket stove and David S and his series of videos come up.

Thanks again,  M

author: @jasonbroom7147
text: The increase of riser height is what's creating the greater inrush of oxygen (that "roar").  For those who think a proper rocket stove creates no more BTU's than a common wood stove, pay close attention to the heat and flame being created by this demonstration.  Put that conflagration to good use with a suitable mass to absorb the heat (soapstone is lovely) and you've got the means of warming a living space using far less fuel.  Which begs the question: what fuel did you use for this demonstration?

author: @mikemojc
text:
Could the vortex be explained by the not-quite-flatness of the ring you made to set the glass on?  
Seems like the air drawing across that piece of metal might start getting a bit of direction as it passes over it, a bit like a rifle barrel, and that direction continues up the tube.  It might be a fine, and now visible, experiment, to add some small directional baffles between the fuel and your chimney tube.

Bonus:  you can now also sprinkle in potassium chloride, magnesium sulfate, copper chloride and such on your fuel for a slightly more visual experience.

replies.author: @johndough8115
replies.text: Air moves like a Fluid.  Its almost impossible to get air to move perfectly straight, without Any vortexes forming.  This is why its so difficult, designing aerodynamic vehicle shapes.  This effect is only amplified, by the initial L-Bend.   Of course, to form a vortex that is reliably repeatable... and in the particular vertical flow and speed that you desire ?   That requires specific design features.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
It is mainly the two major known relevant aerodynamic factors that are producing the vortex: 

1, a drop in pressure, 

and 

2, a smooth surface without perturbances*. 

A third one, being a straight line of travel is not really all that important, but it helps some as well.

*[actually that sort of constitutes 2 separate rules, depending on which physicist you ask, but for this situation we shall leave it as only one rule (some people argue that the roundness is not equivocated with the smoothness, others will insist that they are one in the same thing for this application, I have no opinion either way, personally)]

author: @1kreature
text:
The big problem with rocket stoves is you are making it burn very efficiently by introducing a lot of air, and this causes most of the heat to just rush out the chimney.
To really extract the heat the hot gas needs to pass through a heat exchanger after complete combustion. The colder the exhaust, the better it is at extracting the heat and putting it into the room.
Otherwise it is just burning the fuel for burnings sake.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.text: @davebashford3753 even tempered high temp glass like that will eventually slump to an extent, nothing flipping it 180 every once in a while wouldn't compensate. I'd be more worried about breakage but dang if those flames aren't attractive looking!

author: @Reman1975
text:
That's REALLY spooky that you've uploaded this today. I'm part way through renovating an old Aladdin blue flame paraffin heater, and just this morning I was looking at the chimney and thinking it would be incredibly cool if I could have got a section of heatproof glass to replace that, so it could give off it's glow around the whole room, but I brushed off the idea because I thought it would cost well into 3 figures to get it made.

The chimney section I'd need would be significantly shorter, but a larger diameter than you've found, but if that was only 40 quid, I'm now wondering what a stumpier piece (or suitable diameter offcut) would be? If a length was cheap enough I'd even be happy to take the risk of cutting it to length and diamond drilling the mounting clamp holes myself.............. I think I need to take some measurements and do a bit of googling here. 🤔

text: Beautiful flame.  I assume that it was hot enough to be a heater closer in.  Seems like that soot would only form on the end of the chimney when it was still cold.  It might be thatt a hot enough flame would burn it off on the inside of the tube.  Maybe air injection at the elbow pipe would help?  A "Steam punk" brass or iron guard, like used on a boiler site glass, would look really good, too.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was thinking air injection too - it's a shame the heat haze doesn't really show up because it was impressive - you could actually see the heat it gave off

author: @philipvecchio3292
text:
I wonder what would happen if there was some insulation around the metal.  It might not be getting hot enough for complete combustion, especially as some of the flame hit the top of the glass.

Maybe adding some refractory cement inside or putting it in a sand box to keep heat near the metal.

The increased height for the riser is amazing.  I could see something like that on a patio.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: with the glass we don't really want the temp too high mate

replies.author: @philipvecchio3292
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Is there a way to get more complete combustion without increasing the temperature?  A catalyst or something?

Some of your other videos you used Stainless steel to stay hot and ignite vapors more quickly.

replies.author: @andrewmorris5947
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering thanks for replying.

I currently work in pool construction and am aspiring “RE developer”…

I’m looking to create an amalgamation of utility solutions to make home a self sustaining node simply harnessing the elements.

HVAC/plumbing/power as an interplay the transform one “pressure mediation” into another…

Sand battery as the input temp differential. Integrate water reclamation system, and then use as steam power with turbines and transformers to step power.

Water turbines and steam turbines.
Add a sterling engine or a Tesla invention to the system and your home is a dynamo.

text: One could probably put a regular glass tube around that filled with water in between to double as a gravity fed water heater and to prevent the inner glass from overheating. replies.text: Good idea, but that much heat... I don't think any amount of cooling you could give normal glass would work. The temperature differences would still be to extreme. I'm amazed the tempered glass survived. I've seen rocket stoves glow white hot with just normal wood.
